knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language widely used in MLOps for data science, machine learning, and automation tasks due to its simplicity, flexibility, and extensive libraries. Popular use cases include data preprocessing, model training, and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Importing popular ML libraries: NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn, TensorFlow, Keras — key functions, classes, and methods for data manipulation, modeling, and optimization
Data preprocessing techniques: handling missing values, encoding categorical variables, feature scaling, normalization — using Pandas, Scikit-learn, and NumPy
Model selection and evaluation metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC, confusion matrix — using Scikit-learn, TensorFlow, and Keras
Hyperparameter tuning: GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, Bayesian optimization — using Scikit-learn and Hyperopt
Model deployment: saving models to file, loading models in production environments, model serving with Flask or Django — using Pickle, Joblib, and TensorFlow Serving
Automating tasks with Python scripts: data pipelines, workflows, batch processing — using Airflow, Luigi, and Celery
Working with popular ML frameworks: TensorFlow, Keras, PyTorch — understanding the differences, use cases, and best practices for each framework
Using containerization tools: Docker, Kubernetes — to package and deploy Python applications, including ML models and data pipelines
Monitoring and logging: using libraries like Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly for visualization, and Loguru, structlog for logging

knowledge.what_it_is: Go (also known as Golang) is a statically typed, compiled language developed by Google to build scalable and concurrent systems. It's widely used in MLOps for building data pipelines, model deployment, and infrastructure automation.
what_you_will_learn: Contexts — managing multiple goroutines and their execution order using channels
Channels — safe communication between goroutines using buffered or unbuffered channels
Goroutines — lightweight threads that run concurrently with the main program
Mutexes — synchronizing access to shared resources using mutual exclusion locks
WaitGroups — coordinating multiple goroutines' completion using wait groups
Error handling — using errors as values and propagating them up the call stack
Type safety — ensuring memory safety through explicit type definitions and interfaces
Interface implementation — defining and implementing custom interfaces for structs

knowledge.what_it_is: Bash is a Unix shell and command-line interpreter that allows users to execute commands, navigate directories, and manage files on Linux systems. It is widely used by DevOps engineers and MLOps professionals for automating tasks, managing workflows, and deploying machine learning models.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in Bash scripts, including environment variables and user-defined variables
Conditional statements — writing if-else statements to control the flow of your script based on conditions
Loops — using for loops and while loops to iterate over data or perform repetitive tasks
Functions — defining reusable functions to simplify complex tasks and improve code modularity
File I/O — reading and writing files in Bash, including text files, CSV files, and JSON files
Process management — running background processes, managing process IDs, and killing processes
Error handling — using try-catch blocks to handle errors and exceptions in your scripts

knowledge.what_it_is: Linux is an open-source operating system widely used in MLOps environments, providing a stable and customizable foundation for running machine learning workloads.
what_you_will_learn: Filesystem hierarchy — understanding the Linux directory structure and common paths (/bin, /boot, /dev, /etc, /lib, /proc, /run, /sys, /tmp, /usr)
Process management — managing system processes using tools like ps, top, kill, and bg
User and group management — creating and managing users and groups using useradd, groupadd, and passwd
File permissions and ownership — understanding Linux file permissions (read, write, execute) and ownership models (user, group, others)
Environment variables — setting and using environment variables to configure system settings and application behavior
Package management — installing and managing packages using apt-get, yum, or pip for Python dependencies
System logging — configuring and utilizing Linux system logs (/var/log/messages, /var/log/syslog) for debugging and monitoring
Network configuration — setting up network interfaces, IP addresses, and DNS settings using ifconfig, ip addr, and resolv.conf

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system designed to handle complex projects efficiently, widely used in MLOps pipelines for collaborative development, tracking changes, and reproducibility.
what_you_will_learn: Git repositories — creating and managing central hubs for code collaboration and versioning
Branching strategies — implementing feature branches, release branches, and hotfix branches for efficient parallel development
Merge requests — integrating changes from one branch to another using Git merge or rebase commands
Commit messages — writing clear, concise, and informative commit messages following the Conventional Commits format
Git tags — creating and managing version labels for releases and milestones in MLOPs pipelines
Cherry-picking — selectively applying commits from one branch to another using Git cherry-pick command
Git stash — temporarily storing local changes before switching branches or merging code
Git hooks — customizing the Git workflow with pre-commit, post-commit, and other hooks for automated testing and validation

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, widely used by MLOps teams to manage model repositories, track changes, and collaborate with stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Git — the underlying version control system that GitHub uses, allowing users to track changes, collaborate, and manage different versions of code
GitHub repositories (repos) — creating, managing, and organizing project codebases, including model artifacts and metadata
Branching and merging — using Git branches to isolate development work, merge changes into mainline, and resolve conflicts
Pull requests (PRs) — reviewing and approving changes before they are merged into the main branch, ensuring quality and consistency
GitHub Actions (GHA) — automating workflows, such as building, testing, and deploying models, using YAML files and GitHub's CI/CD capabilities
Model versioning — tracking different versions of models, including metadata and hyperparameters, to enable reproducibility and experimentation
Collaboration and access control — managing team permissions, assigning roles, and controlling access to repositories and sensitive data

knowledge.what_it_is: Data Version Control (DVC) is a tool that helps manage and track changes to data, models, and code in machine learning workflows. It provides a Git-like interface for versioning data, allowing teams to collaborate on ML projects while maintaining reproducibility and auditability.
what_you_will_learn: Versioning datasets — tracking changes to raw data, including metadata and transformations
Model versioning — managing different versions of trained models, including hyperparameters and architecture
Code versioning — controlling changes to ML code, scripts, and notebooks
Data lineage — understanding the origin and transformation history of each dataset component
Change tracking — identifying who made what changes, when, and why
Collaboration features — enabling multiple users to work on the same project simultaneously
Integration with CI/CD pipelines — automating DVC workflows within continuous integration and deployment processes
DVC commands — using `dvc add`, `dvc status`, `dvc diff`, and other commands for data versioning

knowledge.what_it_is: Version control systems are essential tools for MLOps teams to manage code changes, collaborate on projects, and maintain reproducibility. Git is the most widely used version control system, but other alternatives like Mercurial and SVN also exist.
what_you_will_learn: Git — a distributed version control system that allows multiple developers to work together on a project
Repository (repo) structure — understanding how to organize code in a Git repository, including branches, tags, and commits
Branching strategies — learning different branching models like feature branching, release branching, and hotfix branching
Commit messages — writing clear and descriptive commit messages that follow standard guidelines
Merge conflicts — resolving merge conflicts using tools like Git Merge or Git Rebase
Pull requests (PRs) — understanding how to create and review PRs in a collaborative development environment
Code reviews — learning how to conduct thorough code reviews, including best practices for commenting on code
Git hooks — setting up custom scripts that run automatically before or after certain Git events

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud providers AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform (GCP) are essential for MLOps engineers to deploy, manage, and monitor machine learning models. Each provider offers a range of services for data storage, processing, and model deployment.
what_you_will_learn: AWS Sagemaker — a fully managed service for building, training, and deploying ML models, with support for TensorFlow, PyTorch, and scikit-learn
Azure Machine Learning — a cloud-based platform for ML development, deployment, and management, with built-in support for popular frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch
GCP AI Platform — a managed platform for building, deploying, and managing ML models, with support for TensorFlow, PyTorch, and scikit-learn
Cloud storage options: S3 (AWS), Blob Storage (Azure), Cloud Storage (GCP) — understanding the differences in data storage and retrieval between providers
Cloud-based model deployment: AWS SageMaker Autopilot, Azure Machine Learning Model Management, GCP AI Platform Model Deployment — learning how to deploy models on each provider's platform
Monitoring and logging: AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Stackdriver — understanding the importance of monitoring and logging for ML model performance and debugging
Security and compliance: AWS IAM, Azure RBAC, GCP Identity and Access Management — learning about security best practices and compliance requirements for cloud-based ML deployments

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud-native ML services enable machine learning workflows to be deployed, managed, and scaled on cloud platforms like AWS SageMaker, Azure Machine Learning, Google Cloud AI Platform, and others. These services provide a range of features for building, training, deploying, and monitoring ML models, including automated model deployment, hyperparameter tuning, and model explainability.
what_you_will_learn: AWS SageMaker — Amazon's fully managed service for building, training, and deploying ML models, with features like automated model deployment, hyperparameter tuning, and model explainability

Azure Machine Learning — Microsoft's cloud-based platform for building, training, and deploying ML models, with features like automated model deployment, hyperparameter tuning, and model explainability

Google Cloud AI Platform — Google's fully managed service for building, training, and deploying ML models, with features like automated model deployment, hyperparameter tuning, and model explainability
Kubernetes — an open-source container orchestration system for managing cloud-native applications, including ML workloads
Model serving — techniques for deploying trained ML models to production environments, including using APIs, RESTful services, or model servers like TensorFlow Serving
Model monitoring — methods for tracking and analyzing the performance of deployed ML models, including metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and others

knowledge.what_it_is: GPU/Compute Management refers to the process of optimizing and managing computing resources, particularly Graphics Processing Units (GPUs), to accelerate machine learning model training and inference. This is crucial in MLOps as it enables faster deployment and scaling of models.
what_you_will_learn: CUDA — NVIDIA's parallel computing platform for GPU acceleration, including cuDNN for deep learning
TensorFlow XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) — a compiler that translates TensorFlow code into optimized machine code for GPUs
PyTorch CUDA Extension — a set of APIs for writing PyTorch models to run on NVIDIA GPUs
NVIDIA Deep Learning SDK — a comprehensive toolkit for building, training, and deploying deep learning models on NVIDIA GPUs
GPU-accelerated libraries like cuBLAS, cuSOLVER, and cuFFT for linear algebra operations
Containerization of GPU workloads using Docker and NVIDIA's NGC (NVIDIA GPU Cloud) registry
Kubernetes integration with NVIDIA's Device Plugin and GPU-attached Persistent Volumes
Monitoring and logging GPU utilization and performance metrics using tools like nvidia-smi and Prometheus

knowledge.what_it_is: Docker is an open-source containerization platform that allows developers to package, ship, and run applications in containers across environments. It provides a lightweight and portable way to deploy applications, ensuring consistency and reproducibility.
what_you_will_learn: Images — Docker's primary unit of deployment, a read-only template for creating containers
Containers — runtime instances of images, providing isolation and resource allocation
Dockerfile — a text file that contains instructions for building an image from scratch
docker build — command to create an image from a Dockerfile
docker run — command to launch a container from an image
Volumes — directories or files that persist data between container restarts
Networking — Docker's built-in networking capabilities, including bridge networks and overlay networks
Docker Compose — tool for defining and running multi-container applications
docker swarm — orchestration tool for managing clusters of Docker nodes

knowledge.what_it_is: Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration system for automating the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. It was originally designed by Google and is now maintained by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). Kubernetes provides a declarative configuration model, allowing users to define desired states for their applications, rather than specifying how to achieve those states.
what_you_will_learn: Pods — the basic execution unit in Kubernetes, representing a logical host for one or more containers
ReplicaSets — ensuring a specified number of replicas (identical Pods) are running at any given time
Deployments — managing rollouts and rollbacks of new versions of an application
Services — providing network identity and load balancing for accessing applications
Persistent Volumes (PVs) and Claims — persisting data across Pod restarts and rescheduling
Namespaces — organizing resources into logical partitions, improving resource utilization and security
Labels and Selectors — filtering and selecting resources based on metadata attributes
ConfigMaps and Secrets — storing and managing configuration data for applications
DaemonSets — running one or more replicas of a Pod across a cluster
StatefulSets — managing stateful applications, such as databases, with persistent storage
Kubernetes Networking (Calico, Cilium, etc.) — configuring network policies and security groups
Kubernetes Storage (Rook, Longhorn, etc.) — provisioning and managing storage resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Machine learning fundamentals are the building blocks of MLOps, enabling data scientists and engineers to develop, deploy, and maintain accurate and reliable machine learning models. This includes understanding model development, training, evaluation, deployment, and maintenance.
what_you_will_learn: Supervised vs unsupervised learning — understanding the difference between these two fundamental concepts
Regression vs classification — understanding the types of predictive modeling tasks
Linear regression — understanding the basics of linear regression models and their applications
Logistic regression — understanding the basics of logistic regression models and their applications
Decision trees — understanding the basics of decision tree algorithms and their applications
Random forests — understanding the basics of random forest algorithms and their applications
Support vector machines (SVMs) — understanding the basics of SVMs and their applications
K-nearest neighbors (KNN) — understanding the basics of KNN algorithms and their applications
Clustering — understanding the basics of clustering algorithms and their applications
Principal component analysis (PCA) — understanding the basics of PCA and its applications
Model evaluation metrics — understanding metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and mean squared error
Overfitting vs underfitting — understanding the difference between these two common issues in machine learning
Regularization techniques — understanding techniques such as L1 and L2 regularization
Hyperparameter tuning — understanding the importance of hyperparameter tuning for model optimization
Model selection — understanding how to choose the best model for a given problem
Cross-validation — understanding the basics of cross-validation and its applications

knowledge.what_it_is: A data pipeline is a series of automated processes that extract, transform, and load (ETL) data from various sources into a centralized repository, enabling efficient data processing and analysis. In MLOps, data pipelines are crucial for feeding model training data and serving models.
what_you_will_learn: Apache Beam — a unified programming model for both batch and streaming data processing
Apache Airflow — a workflow management system for scheduling and monitoring data pipeline tasks
Apache NiFi — a real-time data integration platform for automating data flows between systems
Data Ingestion using Apache Kafka — designing scalable, fault-tolerant data pipelines for high-volume data streams
Data Transformation with Apache Spark — leveraging Spark's in-memory processing capabilities for efficient data transformation and aggregation
Cloud-based Data Pipelines on AWS Glue or Google Cloud Dataflow — utilizing cloud-native services for serverless data processing and cost optimization

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Spark is an open-source unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing, created by the UC Berkeley AMPLab and maintained by the Apache Software Foundation. It provides high-level APIs in Java, Python, Scala, and R to process data in-memory, enabling fast and efficient data analysis.
what_you_will_learn: Spark Core — understanding Spark's core concepts, including Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs), DataFrames, and Datasets
DataFrames API — using the high-level DataFrame API for structured and semi-structured data processing, including filtering, sorting, and aggregating
Dataset API — working with strongly-typed datasets for efficient in-memory processing of complex data types
Spark SQL — querying structured data using Spark's built-in SQL engine, including support for HiveQL and ANSI SQL
Data Sources — connecting to various data sources, such as HDFS, S3, Cassandra, and JDBC, using Spark's built-in connectors
Job Scheduling — understanding how Spark schedules jobs on a cluster, including the role of the Spark Driver and Executor nodes
Checkpointing — implementing fault-tolerance in Spark applications by periodically saving intermediate results to stable storage

knowledge.what_it_is: A data lake is a centralized repository that allows you to store raw, unprocessed data in its native format, while a data warehouse is a structured and processed version of this data for analytics and reporting. In MLOps, data lakes are used for data ingestion, processing, and storage, while data warehouses provide a governed and curated view of the data.
what_you_will_learn: Data Lake Architecture — designing a scalable and secure data lake using tools like Apache Hadoop, Amazon S3, or Azure Data Lake Storage
Data Ingestion Pipelines — building efficient pipelines for ingesting data from various sources into the data lake using tools like Apache NiFi, AWS Glue, or Azure Data Factory
Data Processing Engines — understanding and working with engines like Apache Spark, Flink, or Hadoop MapReduce for processing large datasets in the data lake
Data Warehousing Concepts — designing and implementing a data warehouse using tools like Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, or Snowflake for analytics and reporting
ETL/ELT Processes — understanding the difference between Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) and Extract-Load-Transform (ELT) processes in data warehousing and data lakes
Data Governance and Security — implementing data governance and security best practices in data lakes and warehouses using tools like Apache Ranger, AWS Lake Formation, or Azure Purview

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Kafka is an open-source distributed streaming platform that enables real-time data processing and integration with various systems, widely used in MLOps pipelines for event-driven architectures.
what_you_will_learn: Kafka clusters — designing and deploying scalable Kafka clusters for high-throughput and low-latency data processing
Topics and partitions — understanding how topics are divided into partitions and how this affects message ordering and retrieval
Producers and consumers — learning about the producer-consumer model, including key concepts like acks, retries, and idempotence
Kafka brokers — managing Kafka broker nodes, including configuration, monitoring, and scaling
Message formats — understanding Avro, JSON, and other message formats used in Kafka for serialization and deserialization
Consumer groups — learning about consumer group management, including rebalancing, offset management, and fault tolerance
Kafka connectors — using pre-built connectors to integrate with various data sources and sinks, such as databases, file systems, and messaging queues
Kafka streams — processing and transforming data in real-time using Kafka Streams API for stateful and stateless computations

knowledge.what_it_is: Data Ingestion Architecture refers to the design and implementation of systems that collect, process, and load data from various sources into a centralized repository or data warehouse. This architecture is critical for MLOps teams to ensure timely and accurate data availability for model training and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Data Sources — understanding the different types of data sources (e.g., databases, APIs, files) and their characteristics
Data Ingestion Tools — familiarizing with popular tools like Apache NiFi, AWS Kinesis, Google Cloud Pub/Sub, and Azure Event Hubs for efficient data collection and processing
Streaming Data Processing — understanding the concepts of stream processing (e.g., event-time processing, windowing) and its applications in real-time analytics and decision-making
Data Transformation and Enrichment — learning about data transformation techniques (e.g., schema-on-read, schema-on-write) and enrichment methods (e.g., data augmentation, feature engineering)
Data Quality and Governance — understanding the importance of data quality checks, data lineage, and metadata management in ensuring data integrity and compliance
Cloud-Native Data Ingestion — exploring cloud-native solutions like AWS Lake Formation, Google Cloud Data Fusion, and Azure Synapse Analytics for scalable and secure data ingestion
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — understanding the principles of EDA and its applications in distributed systems and microservices architecture

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Flink is an open-source, distributed processing engine that provides high-throughput and low-latency data processing capabilities. It is widely used in real-time analytics, event-driven architecture, and big data processing.
what_you_will_learn: Stream Processing — processing data in real-time as it arrives, with support for event-time and watermarking
Dataflow Programming Model — defining data pipelines using a high-level API, with support for parallelism and fault-tolerance
Stateful Stream Processing — managing state across multiple operators, with support for checkpointing and recovery
Table APIs — working with tabular data in Flink, including support for SQL and DataFrames
Kafka Integration — integrating Flink with Apache Kafka for real-time data processing and event-driven architecture
Flink ML — using Flink's built-in machine learning library for model training and deployment
Checkpoints and Recovery — managing checkpoints to ensure fault-tolerance and recovery in case of failures

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Airflow is an open-source workflow management platform that allows users to programmatically schedule and monitor workflows, including data pipelines, machine learning model training, and data processing tasks.
what_you_will_learn: DAGs — Directed Acyclic Graphs: designing and representing workflows as graphs with nodes and edges
Tasks — atomic units of work in Airflow, executed by workers, e.g., Python scripts, shell commands, or SQL queries
Trigger Rules — defining conditions for task execution, e.g., schedule, event-driven, or on-demand
Pools — resource allocation and management for worker nodes, ensuring efficient utilization and scalability
Backfills — re-running failed tasks to maintain data consistency and integrity in workflows
XComs — exchanging data between tasks using a shared key-value store for inter-task communication
Sensor Operators — monitoring external systems or services for changes, triggering task execution when conditions are met

knowledge.what_it_is: MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) refers to the set of practices and tools that enable organizations to manage, deploy, and maintain machine learning models throughout their lifecycle. It involves automating model deployment, monitoring, and maintenance, as well as ensuring model explainability, fairness, and security.
what_you_will_learn: Model Versioning — managing different versions of a model using tools like Git or ModelDB
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines for MLOps — automating testing, building, and deployment of models using tools like Jenkins, Travis CI, or CircleCI
Model Monitoring and Logging — tracking model performance metrics, logs, and errors using tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic
Automated Model Re-training and Deployment — scheduling re-training and redeployment of models based on changing data distributions or business requirements
Model Explainability and Interpretability — techniques for understanding how a model makes predictions, such as SHAP values, LIME, or TreeExplainer
Fairness, Bias, and Ethics in MLOps — methods for detecting and mitigating bias in models, ensuring fairness, and maintaining transparency
Model Security and Anomaly Detection — protecting models from attacks, data poisoning, and other security threats using techniques like adversarial training or anomaly detection
MLOps Tooling and Orchestration — using tools like Apache Airflow, Kubeflow, or MLflow to manage workflows, dependencies, and resources for MLOps pipelines

knowledge.what_it_is: Version control systems are essential tools for MLOps teams to manage code changes, collaborate on projects, and maintain reproducibility. Git is the most widely used version control system, but other popular alternatives include SVN, Mercurial, and Perforce.
what_you_will_learn: Git — a distributed version control system with local repository management, commit history, and branching/merging
GitHub/GitLab — web-based platforms for hosting Git repositories, enabling collaboration, issue tracking, and code reviews
Branching strategies — implementing feature branches, release branches, and hotfix branches to manage parallel development and deployment
Merge requests/Pull requests — submitting changes from one branch to another, with automated testing and review
Code reviews — peer review process for evaluating code quality, security, and maintainability before merging into mainline
Commit messages — writing descriptive commit messages that follow standard guidelines (e.g., imperative mood, present tense)
Git tags — annotating releases or milestones in the Git history with meaningful tags (e.g., v1.0, feature/new-feature)
Git hooks — custom scripts that run at specific points during the Git workflow (e.g., pre-commit, post-merge)

knowledge.what_it_is: CI/CD (Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment) is a software development practice that automates testing, building, and deployment of machine learning models into production environments. It ensures that code changes are validated, tested, and deployed quickly and reliably.
what_you_will_learn: Jenkins — a popular open-source automation server for CI/CD pipelines
Docker — containerization platform for packaging and deploying ML models
Kubernetes — orchestration tool for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containers
GitLab CI/CD — integrated CI/CD tool for automating testing, building, and deployment of code changes
CircleCI — cloud-based CI/CD platform for automating testing, building, and deployment of ML models
Model versioning — tracking and managing different versions of ML models in production
Automated testing — writing unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests for ML models
Automated deployment — using tools like Docker, Kubernetes, or GitLab CI/CD to deploy ML models into production

knowledge.what_it_is: Kubeflow is an open-source platform for distributed training and deployment of machine learning models, built on top of Kubernetes. It provides a simple and scalable way to manage complex workflows and automate the process of model development, testing, and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Kubeflow pipelines — defining and executing ML workflows as code using Kubeflow's pipeline API
Kubeflow notebooks — interactive development environment for data scientists and engineers to experiment with ML models and share results
TFX (TensorFlow Extended) integration — leveraging TFX components to automate the ML workflow, including data validation, model training, and model serving
MLflow integration — using MLflow's tracking API to log experiments, parameters, and metrics within Kubeflow pipelines
Kubernetes resources — understanding how Kubeflow uses Kubernetes resources (pods, services, deployments) to manage ML workflows
GPU and TPU support — utilizing GPUs or TPUs for distributed training of large-scale models within Kubeflow
Monitoring and logging — setting up monitoring and logging tools (e.g. Prometheus, Grafana) to track Kubeflow pipeline performance and troubleshoot issues

knowledge.what_it_is: Experiment tracking and model registry are essential components of MLOps, enabling reproducibility, version control, and collaboration across teams. They provide a centralized platform to track experiments, store models, and manage their lifecycle.
what_you_will_learn: TensorBoard — visualizing experiment metrics and logs for TensorFlow models
Weights & Biases (W&B) — tracking hyperparameters, model performance, and logs for various ML frameworks
MLflow Tracking — storing experiment metadata, parameters, and results in a centralized database
Model Registry with MLflow Model — versioning, registering, and serving models in a production-ready environment
Experiment tracking with DVC (Data Version Control) — managing code, data, and experiments as a single entity
Model Serving with TensorFlow Serving or AWS SageMaker — deploying and managing models in a scalable and secure manner
Hyperparameter tuning with Optuna or Ray Tune — automating hyperparameter search and optimization for ML models

knowledge.what_it_is: MLflow is an open-source platform for managing the end-to-end machine learning lifecycle, from model development to deployment and monitoring. It provides a unified interface for tracking experiments, models, and their dependencies.
what_you_will_learn: Experiment Tracking — creating, running, and comparing experiments with MLflow's built-in UI or APIs
Model Registry — storing, versioning, and deploying models using MLflow's Model Registry feature
Artifact Management — storing and retrieving model artifacts, such as weights, biases, and hyperparameters
Parameter Sweeping — automating hyperparameter tuning with MLflow's parameter sweeping feature
Model Deployment — deploying trained models to cloud or on-premises environments using MLflow's deployment APIs
MLflow Projects — structuring machine learning code and dependencies into reusable projects
MLflow UI — navigating and visualizing experiment results, model performance, and artifact metadata in the web interface

knowledge.what_it_is: Weights & Biases (W&B) is an open-source MLOps platform that helps data scientists and machine learning engineers track, compare, and improve their models. It provides a centralized hub for experiment tracking, model registry, and hyperparameter tuning.
what_you_will_learn: Experiment Tracking — creating and managing experiments with unique IDs, tags, and descriptions
Hyperparameter Tuning — using W&B's built-in tuner or integrating with external libraries like Optuna or Ray Tune
Model Registry — storing and versioning models, including weights, architecture, and metadata
Artifact Management — storing and retrieving model artifacts, such as logs, images, and videos
Metric Tracking — monitoring and visualizing key metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score
Comparison and Analysis — comparing experiments, identifying trends, and analyzing results with W&B's built-in analytics tools
Integration with ML Frameworks — integrating W&B with popular ML frameworks like TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Keras
Customizing the UI — using W&B's API to customize the user interface and create custom dashboards

knowledge.what_it_is: Data Lineage and Feature Stores are critical components of MLOps, enabling data scientists to track data provenance, manage feature engineering, and ensure reproducibility. Feast is an open-source platform for building and serving feature stores.
what_you_will_learn: Feature Store — a centralized repository for storing, managing, and serving features used in machine learning models
Data Lineage — tracking the origin, processing history, and dependencies of data used in ML pipelines
Feast Core — Feast's core components, including the feature store, data lineage, and metadata management
Feast SDKs — client libraries for interacting with Feast from popular programming languages like Python, Java, and Go
Feature Engineering — designing and implementing features that capture meaningful relationships between input variables
Data Quality Monitoring — tracking data quality metrics, such as missing values, outliers, and data drift
Model-Driven Development — using ML models to drive feature engineering and improve model performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Model packaging is the process of bundling and preparing machine learning models for deployment into production environments. It involves creating a standardized format for model artifacts, including weights, biases, and other metadata, to enable efficient and scalable deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Containerization — using Docker to package models with dependencies and create reproducible environments
Model Serving Platforms — deploying models on platforms like TensorFlow Serving, AWS SageMaker, or Azure Machine Learning
Model Packaging Formats — working with formats like TensorFlow SavedModel, ONNX, Core ML, or Keras Model
Artifact Management — using tools like Artipie, ModelHub, or MLflow to manage and track model artifacts across the development lifecycle
Dependency Management — handling dependencies for models, including libraries, frameworks, and custom code
Model Optimization — techniques for reducing model size, improving inference speed, and enhancing overall performance
Model Versioning — strategies for managing multiple versions of a model, including versioning schemes and deployment workflows
CI/CD Pipelines — integrating model packaging into continuous integration and delivery pipelines using tools like Jenkins or GitLab CI/CD

knowledge.what_it_is: FastAPI and Flask are two popular Python web frameworks used for building RESTful APIs, microservices, and web applications. They provide a high-level abstraction over the underlying HTTP protocol, allowing developers to focus on writing business logic without worrying about low-level details.
what_you_will_learn: Creating API endpoints with FastAPI using @app.route() decorator — defining routes for handling HTTP requests
Defining request and response bodies in JSON format using Pydantic models — automatically generating schema and validation
Using dependency injection with FastAPI's Dependency Injection system — managing dependencies between components and services
Implementing authentication and authorization with OAuth2, JWT, or session-based approaches — securing APIs and protecting sensitive data
Configuring and deploying Flask applications using WSGI, Gunicorn, or uWSGI — running Flask apps in production environments
Using FastAPI's built-in support for asynchronous programming with async/await syntax — writing efficient and scalable concurrent code
Handling errors and exceptions with custom error handlers and logging mechanisms — providing meaningful feedback to users and developers
Integrating with popular libraries like SQLAlchemy, pandas, or scikit-learn for data processing and machine learning tasks — building data pipelines and workflows

knowledge.what_it_is: BentoML is an open-source, Python-based model serving and deployment framework that simplifies the process of deploying machine learning models to production. It provides a unified interface for model deployment across various platforms, including cloud providers like AWS, GCP, and Azure.
what_you_will_learn: Model Serving — deploying and managing machine learning models in production using BentoML's high-level APIs
Containerization — packaging models and dependencies into Docker containers for easy deployment and scaling
Model Packaging — creating and managing model artifacts, including weights, configurations, and metadata
Model Deployment — deploying models to various platforms, including cloud providers, Kubernetes, and on-premises environments
Monitoring and Logging — integrating with monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and logging frameworks like Logstash
AutoML Pipelines — automating the deployment of machine learning pipelines using BentoML's AutoML features
Model Versioning — managing multiple versions of models and tracking changes using BentoML's version control system

knowledge.what_it_is: Kserve and Seldon are open-source, cloud-agnostic platforms for serving machine learning (ML) models at scale, providing a unified interface for model deployment, management, and monitoring. They support multiple frameworks, including TensorFlow, PyTorch, and scikit-learn.
what_you_will_learn: KServe — the Kubernetes-based platform for serving ML models, providing automatic scaling, load balancing, and canary releases
Seldon — a cloud-native platform for deploying, managing, and monitoring ML models, with support for multiple frameworks and deployment strategies
Model Serving — understanding how KServe and Seldon enable model serving at scale, including features like caching, queuing, and rate limiting
Kubernetes Operators — learning about the Kubernetes Operator pattern used by KServe to manage and deploy ML models as native Kubernetes resources
Custom Resources (CRs) — understanding how KServe defines custom CRs for ML models, enabling declarative configuration and management
Model Monitoring — learning about the monitoring features provided by Seldon, including metrics collection, logging, and alerting
A/B Testing and Canaries — understanding how KServe and Seldon support A/B testing and canary releases for ML models, enabling safe deployment and experimentation

knowledge.what_it_is: TorchServe is an open-source, scalable, and highly customizable serving framework developed by NVIDIA for deploying PyTorch models in production environments. It provides a simple and efficient way to serve models as web services, supporting various deployment scenarios such as containerization, Kubernetes, and cloud platforms.
what_you_will_learn: Model Serving — deploying PyTorch models as scalable web services using TorchServe
Model Repository — managing model versions and configurations in a centralized repository
Model Loader — dynamically loading models into memory for serving, supporting various file formats and protocols
Model Predictor — executing predictions on incoming requests, handling batch processing and asynchronous workflows
Scalability and Load Balancing — horizontally scaling TorchServe instances to handle increased traffic and load balancing using techniques such as round-robin and least connections
Monitoring and Logging — integrating with popular monitoring tools like Prometheus and Grafana for real-time metrics and logging
Security and Authentication — implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms, such as JWT tokens and role-based access control
Model Updates and Rollbacks — efficiently updating models in production without downtime, including rollbacks to previous versions

knowledge.what_it_is: Shadow and canary deployment are techniques used to minimize risk when deploying machine learning models by rolling out small subsets of users or traffic to test model performance before full rollout.
what_you_will_learn: Shadow deployment — deploying a new model in parallel with the existing one, without affecting production traffic
Canary deployment — gradually increasing traffic to the new model while monitoring its performance and metrics
Traffic splitting — using tools like Istio or Linkerd to split traffic between old and new models based on weights or percentages
Model serving platforms — deploying models through platforms like TensorFlow Serving, AWS SageMaker, or Azure Machine Learning
Monitoring and logging — setting up dashboards and alerts to track model performance metrics such as accuracy, latency, and resource utilization
Rollback strategies — defining procedures for rolling back to the previous model version in case of issues or degraded performance
Model drift detection — monitoring for changes in data distribution or model performance over time to trigger retraining or updates
A/B testing — using statistical methods to compare model performance between different subsets of users or traffic

knowledge.what_it_is: A/B testing, also known as split testing or bucket testing, is a statistical method used to compare two or more versions of a machine learning model to determine which one performs better. It's a crucial aspect of MLOps, ensuring that the deployed models are optimal and meet business requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Experiment design — creating a plan for comparing different ML model variants, including randomization, stratification, and replication
Model serving — deploying multiple versions of an ML model to a production environment using tools like Kubernetes, Docker, or cloud-based services
Evaluation metrics — selecting relevant metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) to compare the performance of different model variants
Hypothesis testing — applying statistical tests (e.g., t-test, ANOVA) to determine if differences in model performance are statistically significant
Confidence intervals — calculating and interpreting confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty in model performance estimates
Model drift detection — monitoring for changes in data distribution or model performance over time using techniques like online learning or incremental learning
Hyperparameter tuning — optimizing hyperparameters of an ML model using techniques like grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization
Model selection — choosing the best-performing ML model variant based on evaluation metrics and statistical significance

knowledge.what_it_is: Model performance monitoring involves tracking and analyzing key metrics to ensure machine learning models are performing as expected, detecting degradation over time, and taking corrective actions to maintain or improve model accuracy.
what_you_will_learn: Metrics — defining and tracking relevant metrics such as precision, recall, F1 score, mean squared error (MSE), and mean absolute error (MAE)
Model evaluation metrics — understanding the difference between metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score in classification problems
Confusion matrix — analyzing the confusion matrix to understand model performance on specific classes or labels
ROC-AUC curve — interpreting the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and area under the curve (AUC) for binary classification problems
Precision-recall curve — understanding the precision-recall curve and its application in imbalanced datasets
Model drift detection — using techniques like statistical process control (SPC) or machine learning-based methods to detect changes in model performance over time
Concept drift detection — identifying changes in underlying data distributions that affect model performance, such as shifts in population demographics or changes in user behavior
Anomaly detection — applying techniques like one-class SVM or autoencoders to identify unusual patterns in model performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Data drift detection is a critical aspect of MLOps, ensuring that machine learning models continue to perform well over time by monitoring changes in data distribution. It involves identifying when model performance degrades due to shifts in underlying data characteristics.
what_you_will_learn: Concept Drift — understanding the difference between concept drift (change in relationships) and data drift (change in distributions)
Drift Detection Metrics — learning about statistical metrics like Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Wasserstein distance, and mean absolute error to detect changes
Anomaly Detection — applying techniques like One-class SVM, Local Outlier Factor, and Isolation Forest to identify unusual data patterns
Autoencoders for Drift Detection — using neural networks to learn data representations and detect deviations from normal behavior
Online Learning and Drift Adaptation — understanding how to adapt models to changing data distributions in real-time
Drift-Aware Model Selection — learning about techniques like ensemble methods, stacking, and meta-learning to select the best model for drift scenarios

knowledge.what_it_is: Concept drift detection is an essential technique in MLOps to identify changes in data distributions over time, ensuring model performance remains optimal. It's crucial for maintaining accurate predictions and preventing model degradation.
what_you_will_learn: Drift types — understanding concept drift (changes in distribution), covariate shift (changes in input distribution), and prior probability shift (changes in class probabilities)
Drift detection methods — learning about statistical tests (e.g., KS test, ADWIN) and machine learning-based approaches (e.g., incremental learning, online anomaly detection)
Autoencoders for drift detection — using neural networks to learn data representations and detect changes in distribution
Anomaly detection techniques — understanding one-class SVM, Local Outlier Factor (LOF), and Isolation Forest algorithms for identifying unusual patterns
Online monitoring with streaming data — implementing real-time drift detection using tools like Apache Flink or Apache Spark Streaming
Model retraining strategies — learning about incremental learning, transfer learning, and ensemble methods to adapt models to changing distributions

knowledge.what_it_is: Evidently AI is an open-source tool for model monitoring and explainability, providing a unified interface to track model performance, fairness, and robustness. It helps MLOps teams identify issues early on and make data-driven decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Model metrics — understanding key performance indicators (KPIs) such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and mean average precision (MAP)
Fairness metrics — analyzing disparate impact, equalized odds, and statistical parity to ensure model fairness
Robustness testing — using techniques like adversarial attacks, noise injection, and data augmentation to evaluate model robustness
Model interpretability — understanding feature importance, partial dependence plots, and SHAP values to explain model decisions
Anomaly detection — identifying unusual patterns in model performance or input data using techniques like One-class SVM and Local Outlier Factor (LOF)
Model drift detection — monitoring changes in model performance over time and adjusting for concept drift, data drift, and distribution shift
Explainability techniques — applying methods like LIME, TreeExplainer, and Anchors to provide transparent explanations for model predictions
Integration with popular ML frameworks — using Evidently AI with TensorFlow, PyTorch, Scikit-learn, and other popular libraries to monitor and explain models

knowledge.what_it_is: Monitoring and observability are crucial components of MLOps, enabling data scientists to track model performance, detect anomalies, and identify areas for improvement. This involves collecting metrics on model predictions, resource utilization, and other relevant KPIs.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus — a popular monitoring system for collecting metrics from various sources
Grafana — a visualization tool for creating dashboards to display Prometheus metrics
New Relic — an observability platform providing insights into application performance and resource utilization
TensorBoard — a built-in TensorFlow tool for visualizing model training and validation metrics
MLflow — an open-source platform for managing the ML lifecycle, including tracking experiments and models
Metrics — defining custom metrics using libraries like TensorBoard or MLflow
Alerting — setting up alerts for anomalies in model performance or resource utilization using tools like Prometheus or New Relic
Visualization — creating visualizations of model performance and resource utilization using tools like Grafana or TensorBoard

knowledge.what_it_is: Terraform is an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool used to manage and provision cloud and on-premises infrastructure. It allows MLOps engineers to define and deploy infrastructure configurations using human-readable configuration files.
what_you_will_learn: Providers — declaring the cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP, etc.) and configuring credentials
Resources — defining infrastructure components such as virtual machines, networks, and storage
Modules — organizing and reusing Terraform configurations for consistent infrastructure deployments
State Management — understanding how Terraform stores and manages state information across multiple runs
Input Variables — passing user-defined values to Terraform configurations using input variables
Output Values — exposing Terraform-managed resource attributes as output values for downstream processes
Terraform Cloud — integrating with Terraform's cloud-based service for collaboration, versioning, and deployment automation

knowledge.what_it_is: AWS CDK (Cloud Development Kit) is an open-source framework that enables developers to define cloud infrastructure in code using programming languages such as TypeScript, Python, or JavaScript. It provides a declarative way to create and manage AWS resources, making it easier to version, reuse, and share cloud infrastructure configurations.
what_you_will_learn: CDK Constructs — building blocks for defining cloud infrastructure, including AWS services like EC2, S3, and RDS
Stacks — logical groups of CDK constructs, used to organize and manage related resources
Environments — environments are used to define the context in which a stack is deployed (e.g., dev, prod)
AWS Provider — the AWS provider is responsible for provisioning and managing AWS resources defined by the CDK
Synthesizing — the process of translating CDK code into CloudFormation templates
CDK Apps — a way to organize and manage multiple stacks within a single application
Contextualization — using environment variables, secrets manager, or other mechanisms to inject configuration values into CDK apps
Advanced Constructs — using constructs like @aws-cdk/core.Construct, @aws-cdk/aws-ec2.Instance, and others for more complex infrastructure definitions

knowledge.what_it_is: In MLOps, declarative and imperative approaches describe how to define and manage machine learning workflows. Declarative focuses on what needs to be done, while imperative specifies how it's done.
what_you_will_learn: Declarative vs Imperative programming paradigms — understanding the implications for ML workflow management
Kubernetes YAML/JSON files — declarative configuration for containerized ML workflows
MLflow projects — defining and managing ML experiments with a declarative API
TensorFlow Configuration Files (TFConfig) — declarative configuration for TensorFlow models
Dask distributed computing — using imperative programming to scale Dask computations
PyTorch Lightning — using imperative programming to define and train PyTorch models
MLflow Model Registry — registering and managing ML models with a declarative API

knowledge.what_it_is: Environmental management in MLOps refers to the practices and tools used to manage the environmental impact of machine learning models, including carbon footprint, energy consumption, and e-waste generation. It involves monitoring and optimizing model performance while reducing its ecological footprint.
what_you_will_learn: Carbon accounting — tracking and quantifying the carbon emissions associated with training and deploying ML models
Energy efficiency optimization — techniques for minimizing energy consumption during model training and inference, such as using low-precision arithmetic or model pruning
Model serving and deployment on cloud platforms with environmental considerations (e.g., AWS Sagemaker, Google Cloud AI Platform)
Containerization and orchestration tools like Docker and Kubernetes for efficient resource utilization
Monitoring and logging frameworks for tracking model performance and environmental metrics (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana)
Model interpretability techniques to understand how models make decisions and identify areas for improvement
Automated testing and validation of ML models using tools like Pytest and Hypothesis

knowledge.what_it_is: Explainable AI (XAI) refers to techniques and tools that provide insights into how a machine learning model makes predictions, enabling trustworthiness, accountability, and transparency. XAI is crucial for MLOps teams to ensure models are fair, unbiased, and reliable.
what_you_will_learn: SHAP values — visualizing feature importance using SHapley Additive exPlanations
LIME explanations — generating local interpretable model-agnostic explanations with Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations
Saliency maps — highlighting input features contributing to predictions with Saliency Maps
Feature importance scores — calculating and visualizing feature contributions using techniques like permutation importance or recursive feature elimination
Model-agnostic interpretability methods — applying techniques like LIME, SHAP, or TreeExplainer to any machine learning model
Model-specific interpretability methods — leveraging built-in explainability tools for models like linear regression, decision trees, or neural networks
Partial dependence plots — visualizing the relationship between a single feature and the predicted output using Partial Dependence Plots
Permutation feature importance — evaluating feature importance by permuting feature values and measuring prediction impact

knowledge.what_it_is: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) is an open-source Python library that provides a unified approach to explain the output of machine learning models. It assigns feature importance scores based on the SHapley value, a concept from cooperative game theory.
what_you_will_learn: Feature attribution — understanding how SHAP explains model predictions by assigning importance scores to input features
SHapley values — calculating the contribution of each feature to the model's output using the SHapley formula and the coalition value
Partial dependence plots (PDPs) — visualizing the relationship between a single feature and the model's output
Force plots — displaying the effect of individual features on the model's predictions
SHAP values for multiple models — combining explanations from different models using techniques like stacking or ensembling
Handling categorical variables — understanding how SHAP handles categorical features with multiple categories
Interpretability metrics — evaluating the quality and consistency of SHAP explanations using metrics like mean absolute importance (MAI) or permutation feature importance (PFI)
SHAP for model selection — using SHAP to compare and select between different machine learning models

knowledge.what_it_is: Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) is an algorithmic explanation method that generates interpretable models to explain predictions made by complex machine learning models. LIME is a model-agnostic approach, meaning it can be applied to any machine learning model without modifying its architecture.
what_you_will_learn: Local Surrogate Models — creating simple, interpretable models that mimic the behavior of the original model in a specific region
Model Agnostic Explanations — generating explanations for predictions made by any machine learning model, regardless of its complexity or architecture
Feature Importance Scores — calculating the contribution of each feature to the predicted outcome using LIME's weighted explanation
Partial Dependence Plots (PDPs) — visualizing the relationship between a single input feature and the predicted outcome for a specific instance
SHAP Values — assigning a value to each feature based on its contribution to the predicted outcome, allowing for more nuanced explanations
LIME API Integration — using LIME's Python API to integrate explanation generation into existing machine learning workflows

knowledge.what_it_is: Model compression and quantization are techniques used to reduce the size of machine learning models while maintaining their accuracy, making them more deployable on edge devices or reducing inference latency. This is crucial for MLOps as it enables faster model deployment, reduced storage costs, and improved model explainability.
what_you_will_learn: Weight pruning — removing redundant weights to reduce model size without affecting accuracy
Quantization-aware training (QAT) — retraining models with quantized weights during training for better post-training quantization results
Post-training quantization (PTQ) — converting float32 models to int8 or other lower-precision formats after training, reducing inference latency and memory usage
Knowledge distillation — transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model, enabling efficient compression and improved accuracy
Model pruning with magnitude-based methods — removing weights based on their magnitude, preserving the most important connections for better performance
Model pruning with sensitivity-based methods — removing weights based on their sensitivity to input data, reducing overfitting and improving robustness
Quantization-aware neural architecture search (QANAS) — searching for optimal model architectures that are quantization-friendly from the start
Model compression with Huffman coding — encoding model weights using variable-length codes to reduce storage requirements
